town.name: Clawson
town.tagline: The Little City with a Big Heart

bodies.name: City Council
bodies.role: Final authority on ordinances, budget, and rezonings.

bodies.name: Planning Commission
bodies.role: Holds hearings and recommends land-use decisions to Council.

bodies.name: Zoning Board of Appeals
bodies.role: Decides variances and appeals of zoning decisions.

bodies.name: Downtown Development Authority
bodies.role: Leads downtown through the Main Street program: events, storefront improvements, and small-business grants.
bodies.takeAction: Join a Main Street committee, or ask about a storefront improvement grant.
bodies.note: Board members are appointed by Council. The city's downtown page lists the current roster.

bodies.name: Parks & Recreation Board
bodies.role: Guides the city's parks, recreation programs, and the Hunter Community & Senior Center.
bodies.takeAction: Propose or volunteer for a program, or share your thoughts on a park plan. A seat is open now.

bodies.name: Blair Memorial Library Board
bodies.location: Blair Memorial Library, 416 N. Main St.
bodies.role: Governs the Blair Memorial Library, its budget, policies, and services.
bodies.takeAction: Attend a board meeting, or support the library through the Friends of the Library.
bodies.note: The library has served Clawson since 1929.

bodies.name: Traffic & Safety Board
bodies.role: Advises Council on traffic, parking, and pedestrian safety around the city.
bodies.takeAction: Bring a traffic or safety concern to a meeting, or apply for the open seat.

bodies.name: Historical Commission
bodies.cadence: 7:00 PM on the 2nd Tuesday of January, April, July, and October
bodies.role: Keeps Clawson's history and runs the Historical Museum.
bodies.takeAction: Volunteer, share an old photo or story, or attend a quarterly meeting.

bodies.name: Board of Review
bodies.cadence: Convenes each March to hear assessment appeals, with limited July and December sessions
bodies.role: Hears appeals of a property's assessed value, the figure that drives your tax bill.
bodies.takeAction: File an appeal with the assessor in March and bring evidence such as recent comparable sales.

bodies.name: Board of Ethics
bodies.cadence: Annually, or as needed when a matter arises
bodies.role: Issues written advisory opinions on ethics questions. Sitting elected officials cannot serve on it.
bodies.takeAction: File a complaint or request an advisory opinion through the City Clerk.

bodies.name: Compensation Commission
bodies.role: Recommends the salaries of the city's elected officials.
bodies.takeAction: A seat is open now. Apply through the City Clerk.

bodies.name: Elections Commission
bodies.cadence: As required to administer city elections
bodies.role: Oversees the conduct of the city's elections.
bodies.takeAction: Most of this work is set by law. Election workers are recruited through the City Clerk's office.

officials.name: Susan Moffitt
officials.note: Elected mayor Nov 4, 2025 (previously Mayor Pro Tem).

source.label: Oakland County unofficial results, via C&G Newspapers

officials.name: Scott Tinlin
officials.note: Won a 2-year term; served on Council 2022–2023.

officials.name: Adam Duberstein
officials.note: Appointed by Council in May 2026 to fill a vacant seat.

source.label: C&G Newspapers — Adam Duberstein to fill vacant Clawson City Council seat
City of Clawson — Approved City Council minutes, June 16, 2026

threads.title: 340 N. Main Street — rezoning
threads.summary: A former church on a narrow ~1.5-acre lot, proposed for rezoning from Office (O-1) and Single-Family (R-1/R-2) to Core Residential (CR) to allow townhome-style units. A long back-and-forth between the Planning Commission and City Council.
threads.status: Resubmitted — back before the Planning Commission

timeline.event: Planning Commission recommends rezoning 4–3. (A 5-vote threshold is needed to enact at the commission level, so it advances to Council as a recommendation.)
Council sends the request back to the Planning Commission after residents raise building-height and privacy concerns.
On the revised application, the Planning Commission approves the rezoning 6–1; staff finds it consistent with the adopted master plan and future land use map.

Council DENIES the rezoning 3–2. Roll call — Millan: no; Scott: no; Shepherd: no; Moffitt: yes; Anderson: yes. Residents and members raised access, scale, and enforcement concerns; some floated buying the site for green space.
The applicant resubmits; the request is scheduled to go before the Planning Commission again.

source.label: City of Clawson — Development in Clawson

items.title: 340 N. Main Street — rezoning (resubmitted)
items.summary: Public hearing on the resubmitted request to rezone the former church site to Core Residential (CR).

source.label: City of Clawson — Development in Clawson

items.title: Zoning Ordinance text amendment
items.summary: Public hearing on a proposed amendment to the zoning ordinance text.

source.label: City of Clawson — Development in Clawson

items.title: Consent agenda & minutes
items.summary: Approval of prior minutes and routine consent items.

items.title: 340 N. Main Street — rezoning (resubmitted)
items.summary: The Commission held the public hearing on the resubmitted request to rezone the former church site at 340 N. Main to Core Residential. After the hearing, it voted 5 to 1 to recommend approval to the City Council. The Council makes the final decision at a later meeting.

items.title: Zoning Ordinance text amendment
items.summary: The Commission held a public hearing on a proposed change to the zoning ordinance text covering accessory dwelling units. No vote was taken; the item was carried to the next meeting for more discussion.

companion.text:
The City Council met on June 16, 2026. Here is a plain look at what happened.

The meeting opened with awards from the Police Department. The department recognized members for their service. The approved minutes name each person honored and the reason for each award.

City staff gave a presentation about the city's sewer system and a possible bond for infrastructure work. Crews have been running cameras through the sewer lines to check their condition, which is called a CCTV sewer inspection. A bond is money the city would borrow now and pay back over time. During the discussion, some residents asked for clearer information about what a bond would cost. They wanted to understand the cost before the city moves ahead. The Council heard those questions.

The Council then handled four items of new business.

First, the Council approved budget amendments for the fourth quarter of the 2025 to 2026 fiscal year. These are updates to the current year's budget. The vote was 7 to 0.

Second, the Council named the city's representatives to SEMCOG, the regional planning group for Southeast Michigan. Council member Adam Duberstein was named the delegate. City Manager Joseph Rheker was named the alternate. Council member Duberstein was the person being appointed, so he abstained from that vote. The vote was 6 to 0 with one abstention.

Third, the Council approved a legal services agreement so the city can take part in a drinking-water settlement with 3M and DuPont over PFAS. PFAS are a group of long-lasting chemicals that can show up in water. The vote was 7 to 0.

Fourth, the Council received and filed the city's goals and measurables for the 2026 to 2027 year. Receiving and filing means the Council formally accepted the document into the record. The vote was 7 to 0.

Later in the meeting, the Council voted to go into a closed session. The reason was pending litigation, AWA Investment LLC versus the City of Clawson. State law allows a closed session for this purpose under the Open Meetings Act, section 15.268(h). Closed sessions are private by law, so nothing from that discussion is public.

After the closed session, the Council returned to open session and took one public vote. The Council directed the City Attorney to move ahead with a settlement in a property tax case, Michigan Tax Tribunal docket 25-001597. The vote was 7 to 0.

Mayor Susan Moffitt and City Clerk Machele Kukuk signed the minutes, so this is the approved record of the meeting.

source.label: Drafted by Commonwealth from the approved City Council minutes and meeting video for June 16, 2026

items.title: Police Department awards and recognitions

items.summary: Staff gave a presentation about the city's sewer system and a possible bond for infrastructure work. Crews have been running cameras through the sewer lines to check their condition, which is called a CCTV sewer inspection. A bond is money the city would borrow now and pay back over time. During the discussion, some residents asked for clearer information about what a bond would cost. The Council heard those questions.

items.title: Consent agenda (CM 06-145-26)
items.summary: The Council approved the consent agenda in a single vote. It included the minutes of the June 2 meeting, the appointments of Zablocki and McDonald to SOCWA and SOCRRA, and the list of bills totaling $1,099,806.22. A consent agenda groups routine items into one vote.

source.label: City of Clawson — Approved City Council minutes, June 16, 2026

items.summary: The Council approved budget amendments for the fourth quarter of the 2025 to 2026 fiscal year. These are updates to the current year's budget.

items.summary: The Council named the city's representatives to SEMCOG, the regional planning group for Southeast Michigan. Council member Adam Duberstein was named the delegate. City Manager Joseph Rheker was named the alternate. Council member Duberstein was the person being appointed as delegate, so he abstained from the vote.

items.summary: The Council approved a legal services agreement so the city can take part in a drinking-water settlement with 3M and DuPont over PFAS. PFAS are a group of long-lasting chemicals that can show up in water.

items.title: Motion to enter closed session (CM 06-151-26)
items.summary: The Council voted to go into a closed session to discuss pending litigation, AWA Investment LLC versus the City of Clawson. State law allows a closed session for this purpose under the Open Meetings Act, section 15.268(h). Closed sessions are private by law, so nothing from the discussion is part of the public record.

source.label: City of Clawson — Approved City Council minutes, June 16, 2026

items.title: Direct the City Attorney on the Michigan Tax Tribunal settlement (CM 06-153-26)
items.summary: After the closed session, the Council returned to open session and took one public vote. The Council directed the City Attorney to move ahead with a stipulated settlement in a property tax case, Michigan Tax Tribunal docket 25-001597.

records.title: 121 Renshaw — the city's senior apartments
records.body: The City of Clawson owns and operates a 15-unit senior apartment complex at 121 Renshaw (fourteen 1-bedroom units and one efficiency; residents 60+). Decisions about it — leases, budget, any sale or change — run through the city.

source.label: City of Clawson — Renshaw Senior Apartments

records.title: Your water quality report (2024)
records.body: Clawson publishes an annual water quality report (CCR) for public water supply MI0001440. It lists what was tested and what was found — the plain-language version, linked to the official report.

source.label: City of Clawson — Annual Water Quality Report

records.title: 210 E. 14 Mile — Renshaw's Lounge rear-setback variance
records.body: A variance request for a rear setback at 210 E. 14 Mile Road (Renshaw's Lounge) went before the Zoning Board of Appeals in October 2025.

source.label: City of Clawson — Development in Clawson

infrastructureProjects.title: Main Street repaving, downtown block
infrastructureProjects.summary: Crews are repaving this stretch of N. Main Street. Expect one lane to be closed during work hours, so downtown traffic may be slow at times. You can still reach the shops and driveways along the block.
infrastructureProjects.location: N. Main Street between 14 Mile and Elmwood

source.label: Illustrative example, not a real project

infrastructureProjects.title: Water main replacement, west side
infrastructureProjects.summary: The city plans to replace an aging water main under these streets. A water main is the pipe that carries water to your home. During the work, your water may be shut off for a few hours at a time. The city gives affected homes notice before any shutoff.
infrastructureProjects.timeline: Planned for next year, pending budget approval.

source.label: Illustrative example, not a real project

infrastructureProjects.title: Sidewalk repair, Fisher Court
infrastructureProjects.summary: This sidewalk repair is finished. The uneven sections near the community center have been replaced, so the walk is smooth and safe again.
infrastructureProjects.location: Fisher Court near the community center

source.label: Illustrative example, not a real project
